topic: Two friends plan separate birthday parties on the same day
raw_output:
[story 1]

The calendar glared at me, a stark reminder of the day. Sarah’s birthday was Saturday, and Mark’s was the same. A knot tightened in my chest, a familiar, heavy pressure that made every breath feel shallow. I stared at the half-finished drawing on my desk, the lines clumsy and uninspired. It felt like I was an afterthought, a smudge on someone else’s perfect picture. I knew what I had to do. I needed space, a clean break from the expectation of being *needed*. I pulled out my phone, my fingers trembling slightly as I typed out a message to Sarah, a carefully worded invitation to her celebration. As I sent it, a strange lightness washed over me, a momentary reprieve. Then, the dread returned, a cold seep into my bones, realizing I had just traded one kind of anxiety for another.

[story 2]

"It's just easier if we don't overlap," I mumbled into my lukewarm coffee, tracing the condensation on the mug. The silence in my apartment was loud, amplifying the incessant, critical voice in my head. Seeing Mark’s party—the vibrant plans, the laughter I’d only ever heard him conjure—made my stomach clench. I felt like a poorly tuned instrument, incapable of contributing anything genuine to such a joyous occasion. I decided I would focus entirely on my own small, isolated bubble. I spent the next hour meticulously planning my own, smaller gathering, a quiet affair where I could control every detail, every interaction. The effort felt exhausting, a monumental task undertaken by someone who didn't feel like they deserved the effort in the first place.

[story 3]

The sheer weight of the day pressed down on me, making the floorboards feel unsteady beneath my feet. Every notification buzzed with the phantom sound of congratulations I wasn't receiving. I watched the sunlight shift across the dusty windowpane, each beam illuminating the imperfections in the room, in my reflection. I felt a deep, hollow ache behind my ribs, a constant dull throb that made simple tasks, like picking up my keys, feel like scaling a mountain. I called Mark, my voice sounding thin and reedy. "I can't make it," I managed, the words tasting like ash. The relief that followed was immediate, sharp, but it was quickly replaced by a sinking feeling. I was retreating, sealing myself off, convinced that my presence, even as an absent one, was an imposition.
